title: The Philosophy of Time
summary: A narrated essay surveys major philosophical theories of time — presentism, eternalism, and the growing block universe — and explores the tension between the subjective experience of temporal passage and the picture of time offered by modern physics.
body: Of all the phenomena that philosophy has attempted to illuminate, time is perhaps the most intimate and the most resistant to analysis. We live inside it entirely; we cannot step outside it to gain perspective; and yet when we try to say with precision what it is, our concepts seem to dissolve under examination. Augustine's observation in the Confessions — 'What then is time? If no one asks me, I know; if I wish to explain it to one who asks, I know not' — remains, after sixteen centuries, as apt a summary of the problem as any. Philosophers broadly distinguish between two questions about time: the metaphysical question of what time fundamentally is, and the phenomenological question of how time is experienced. The two questions are connected but not identical, and some of the most productive tensions in the philosophy of time arise from the gap between them. On the metaphysical side, the principal competing theories are presentism, eternalism, and the growing block universe. Presentism holds that only the present moment genuinely exists; the past and future are real in some derivative sense — we can refer to them meaningfully, recall the past, and anticipate the future — but they do not exist as concrete entities in the way that present objects do. This view has strong intuitive support: it captures the sense that the past is gone and the future has not yet arrived. Eternalism, by contrast, holds that past, present, and future are equally real. On this view, the distinction between past, present, and future is analogous to the distinction between here and there in space: a matter of perspective rather than of objective ontological difference. The philosopher J.M.E. McTaggart introduced a famous distinction between what he called the A-series — the ordering of events as past, present, or future — and the B-series — the ordering of events as earlier, simultaneous, or later. Eternalists typically favour the B-series, arguing that the A-series properties of 'being past' or 'being future' are indexical: they describe the event's relation to the speaker's temporal location, just as 'here' describes a spatial relation to the speaker's position. The growing block universe, associated with the philosopher C.D. Broad, offers a middle position. On this view, both the present and the past exist, but the future does not. The universe is a block of spacetime that grows as new events occur and become permanent features of the past. This view preserves the intuition that the past is fixed and real while accommodating the sense that the future is genuinely open — that what has not yet happened is not merely unknown but not yet determined. Physics complicates all three views. Einstein's special theory of relativity established that simultaneity is not absolute: two events that are simultaneous in one frame of reference need not be simultaneous in another. This finding strongly supports the B-series picture of time and poses serious difficulties for presentism, since 'the present moment' turns out not to be a well-defined global concept. The present of an observer on Earth and the present of a hypothetical observer in a distant galaxy moving at relativistic velocity may differ by thousands of years. And yet the phenomenology of time is stubbornly at odds with the physicist's picture. We do not experience time as a dimension analogous to space; we experience it as flowing, as having a direction — from past to future rather than the reverse — and as having a privileged moment, the present, which we inhabit and which moves forward. Philosophers call this the 'passage of time', and it is one of the most analysed and disputed topics in the field. Some, like Huw Price, argue that the appearance of temporal flow is a cognitive illusion: the direction of time is no more intrinsic to the universe than the direction 'up' is intrinsic to space; it is a feature of our perspective, not of reality. Others maintain that any adequate metaphysics of time must account for the reality of temporal becoming — the genuine ontological difference between what is happening now and what has already happened. The philosophy of memory adds a further dimension. Our access to the past is not through direct perception but through memory, which is notoriously reconstructive rather than archival. Neuroscientists have established that remembering is an active process of reconstruction, not passive retrieval. If our sense of a personal past is constructed rather than simply retrieved, the relationship between subjective temporal experience and the objective structure of time becomes even more fraught. What makes the philosophy of time so persistently difficult is that it sits at the intersection of metaphysics, physics, and phenomenology — three domains using different methods and different standards of evidence. A fully adequate theory of time would have to be coherent across all three. We do not yet have such a theory, and some philosophers doubt we ever will. But the attempt remains among the most demanding and rewarding exercises philosophy can offer.

annotations.meaningVi: thuyết hiện tại (chỉ hiện tại tồn tại)
annotations.exampleEn: Presentism holds that only present entities are real; past and future do not exist.

annotations.meaningVi: thuyết vĩnh cửu (quá khứ, hiện tại, tương lai đều thực)
annotations.exampleEn: Eternalism treats the past and future as equally real as the present.

annotations.meaningVi: vũ trụ khối đang tăng trưởng
annotations.exampleEn: The growing block universe holds that the past and present exist but the future does not.

annotations.meaningVi: thuộc về hiện tượng học
annotations.exampleEn: A phenomenological approach to time focuses on how it is experienced from the inside.

annotations.meaningVi: Chuỗi-A (quá khứ/hiện tại/tương lai) và Chuỗi-B (trước/đồng thời/sau)
annotations.exampleEn: McTaggart's A-series describes time in terms of past, present, and future; the B-series uses earlier, simultaneous, and later.

annotations.meaningVi: chỉ thị (từ có nghĩa phụ thuộc vào ngữ cảnh)
annotations.exampleEn: 'Here', 'now', and 'I' are indexical expressions whose meaning depends on who utters them and when.

annotations.meaningVi: sự khác biệt về bản thể luận
annotations.exampleEn: Whether the future has a different ontological status from the past is a central question in the philosophy of time.

annotations.meaningVi: thuyết tương đối hẹp
annotations.exampleEn: Einstein's special theory of relativity showed that time passes at different rates for observers moving at different speeds.

annotations.meaningVi: tính đồng thời
annotations.exampleEn: The relativity of simultaneity means two observers may disagree about whether events happened at the same time.

annotations.meaningVi: hệ quy chiếu
annotations.exampleEn: In physics, measurements of time depend on the observer's frame of reference.

annotations.meaningVi: vận tốc tương đối (gần tốc độ ánh sáng)
annotations.exampleEn: At relativistic velocity, time dilation becomes measurable and significant.

annotations.meaningVi: sự trôi qua của thời gian
annotations.exampleEn: The subjective experience of the passage of time varies greatly depending on attention and emotional state.

annotations.meaningVi: sự trở thành theo thời gian
annotations.exampleEn: Temporal becoming refers to the process by which future events genuinely come into existence.

annotations.meaningVi: ảo giác nhận thức
annotations.exampleEn: Some philosophers argue that the flow of time is a cognitive illusion generated by the mind.

annotations.meaningVi: ký ức tái cấu trúc
annotations.exampleEn: Reconstructive memory means that we partly create our recollections each time we remember.

annotations.meaningVi: thích hợp, đúng lúc
annotations.exampleEn: The professor found an apt analogy to explain the concept to her students.

annotations.meaningVi: không-thời gian
annotations.exampleEn: In Einstein's physics, space and time are unified into a four-dimensional spacetime continuum.

annotations.meaningVi: siêu hình học
annotations.exampleEn: Metaphysics addresses the most fundamental questions about what exists and the nature of reality.

annotations.meaningVi: đầy phức tạp về mặt triết học
annotations.exampleEn: The relationship between mind and body remains philosophically fraught despite centuries of debate.

annotations.meaningVi: sự ủng hộ trực giác
annotations.exampleEn: The view that causes precede effects has strong intuitive support.

annotations.meaningVi: thuộc về bản chất, cố hữu
annotations.exampleEn: Some philosophers argue that temporal direction is intrinsic to reality, not a product of perspective.

grammarNotes.title: Adverbial 'By contrast' and Discourse Markers for Structuring Comparison
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn phong học thuật, các từ nối như 'by contrast', 'on this view', 'on the other hand' giúp tổ chức sự so sánh giữa các lý thuyết cạnh tranh. Ví dụ: 'Eternalism, by contrast, holds that past, present, and future are equally real.' Những từ nối này báo hiệu cho người đọc/nghe rằng một quan điểm đối lập đang được trình bày. Sử dụng chúng đúng cách là dấu hiệu của văn bản học thuật thành thục.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Discourse markers such as 'by contrast', 'on this view', 'on the other hand', and 'in turn' signal transitions between competing positions in academic writing. 'Eternalism, by contrast, holds…' clearly marks a shift from the previously described view (presentism) to an alternative. Correct use of these markers allows writers to structure complex multi-position comparisons without confusing the reader about which theory is under discussion.

grammarNotes.title: Reported Thought with 'holds that', 'maintains that', 'argues that'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn bản triết học và học thuật, các động từ báo cáo như 'holds that', 'maintains that', 'argues that', 'contends that' được dùng để trình bày quan điểm của một lý thuyết hoặc triết gia. Đây là cách chính xác để phân biệt quan điểm của tác giả với quan điểm được trích dẫn. Ví dụ: 'Presentism holds that only the present moment genuinely exists.' Lưu ý rằng mệnh đề sau 'that' dùng thì hiện tại (present simple) dù lý thuyết có thể có từ lâu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Holds that', 'maintains that', and 'argues that' introduce a position or claim attributed to a theory or thinker. They follow a present-tense pattern regardless of when the position was proposed: 'Presentism holds that only the present moment genuinely exists.' This convention treats theories as timeless assertions. It differs from narrative reporting ('said that', 'wrote that') and signals that the claim is a standing position, not a historical event.

translationVi: Trong tất cả các hiện tượng mà triết học đã cố gắng làm sáng tỏ, thời gian có lẽ là gần gũi nhất và khó phân tích nhất. Câu quan sát của Augustine — 'Vậy thì thời gian là gì? Nếu không ai hỏi tôi, tôi biết; nếu tôi muốn giải thích cho người hỏi, tôi không biết' — vẫn còn phù hợp sau mười sáu thế kỷ. Bài viết phân biệt giữa câu hỏi siêu hình học về thời gian là gì và câu hỏi hiện tượng học về cách thời gian được trải nghiệm. Ba lý thuyết chính được khảo sát: thuyết hiện tại (chỉ hiện tại tồn tại), thuyết vĩnh cửu (quá khứ, hiện tại và tương lai đều thực như nhau), và vũ trụ khối đang tăng trưởng của C.D. Broad. Thuyết tương đối hẹp của Einstein gây khó khăn cho thuyết hiện tại vì tính đồng thời không phải là tuyệt đối. Tuy nhiên, hiện tượng học về thời gian — trải nghiệm của chúng ta về sự trôi qua, phương hướng và khoảnh khắc hiện tại đặc quyền — mâu thuẫn với bức tranh của nhà vật lý. Triết học về ký ức bổ sung thêm rằng việc nhớ lại là tái cấu trúc chứ không phải lưu trữ, làm phức tạp hơn mối quan hệ giữa kinh nghiệm thời gian chủ quan và cấu trúc khách quan của thời gian.
criticalThinkingQuestion: The essay presents the 'passage of time' as both a universal human experience and a possible cognitive illusion. If temporal flow is indeed an illusion, what would that mean for concepts such as regret, anticipation, and planning? Can a philosophy of time that denies the reality of temporal passage give an adequate account of human experience?

questions.prompt: What does the essay identify as the two main questions philosophers ask about time?
options: Whether time is finite or infinite, and whether it can be reversed
The metaphysical question of what time is and the phenomenological question of how time is experienced
Whether God created time and whether time travel is possible
The physical measurement of time and its biological perception
questions.explanation: 'Philosophers broadly distinguish between two questions about time: the metaphysical question of what time fundamentally is, and the phenomenological question of how time is experienced.'
questions.hint: One question is about nature; the other is about experience.

questions.prompt: What does presentism claim about past, present, and future?
options: Only the present moment genuinely exists; past and future are not concrete entities
The future is the only truly real time because it is yet to come
Past, present, and future are all illusions
questions.explanation: 'Presentism holds that only the present moment genuinely exists… they do not exist as concrete entities in the way that present objects do.'
questions.hint: Which temporal zone does presentism privilege?

questions.prompt: What analogy does eternalism use to explain the distinction between past, present, and future?
options: The difference between solid, liquid, and gaseous states of matter
The layers of an archaeological dig
The distinction between here and there in space
questions.explanation: 'the distinction between past, present, and future is analogous to the distinction between here and there in space: a matter of perspective rather than of objective ontological difference.'

questions.prompt: What did McTaggart call the ordering of events as past, present, or future?
questions.explanation: 'The philosopher J.M.E. McTaggart introduced a famous distinction between what he called the A-series — the ordering of events as past, present, or future…'
questions.hint: Which letter series is associated with past/present/future?

questions.prompt: What does the essay mean by describing A-series properties as 'indexical'?
options: They appear in the index of a philosophy textbook
They are universal and apply equally to all observers
They describe the event's relation to the speaker's temporal location, like 'here' for space
They can be measured with precision using physical instruments
questions.explanation: 'the A-series properties of 'being past' or 'being future' are indexical: they describe the event's relation to the speaker's temporal location, just as 'here' describes a spatial relation to the speaker's position.'
questions.hint: What does 'here' describe that 'past/future' also describes?

questions.prompt: What is the 'growing block universe' view, associated with C.D. Broad?
options: The past, present, and future all exist in equal measure
Only the present exists; all else is illusion
Both past and present exist but the future does not; the universe grows as new events occur
questions.explanation: 'On this view, both the present and the past exist, but the future does not. The universe is a block of spacetime that grows as new events occur and become permanent features of the past.'
questions.hint: What temporal zones exist in this view?

questions.prompt: What problem does Einstein's special theory of relativity pose for presentism?
options: It shows that the past can be changed under certain conditions
It establishes that simultaneity is not absolute, so 'the present moment' is not a well-defined global concept
It proves that time travel is theoretically possible
It demonstrates that the future already exists in higher dimensions
questions.explanation: 'Einstein's special theory of relativity established that simultaneity is not absolute… 'the present moment' turns out not to be a well-defined global concept.'
questions.hint: What property of time does relativity undermine?

questions.prompt: What concrete example does the essay give to illustrate the relativistic problem for presentism?
options: A clock on a fast train runs slower than one at a station
Observers on different planets age at different rates
The present of an observer on Earth and one in a distant galaxy at relativistic velocity may differ by thousands of years
GPS satellites require relativistic corrections to remain accurate
questions.explanation: 'The present of an observer on Earth and the present of a hypothetical observer in a distant galaxy moving at relativistic velocity may differ by thousands of years.'

questions.prompt: How does the essay characterise our subjective experience of time compared to space?
options: We experience time as flowing with direction and a privileged present, unlike space
We experience time and space identically as undifferentiated dimensions
We experience time as static, like a map spread before us
Our experience of time is more reliable than our experience of space
questions.explanation: 'We do not experience time as a dimension analogous to space; we experience it as flowing, as having a direction… and as having a privileged moment, the present, which we inhabit and which moves forward.'
questions.hint: What three qualities do we experience in time that we do not in space?

questions.prompt: What does Huw Price argue about the direction of time?
options: The direction of time is an intrinsic feature of the universe
The appearance of temporal flow is a cognitive illusion; direction of time is a feature of perspective, not reality
Time can flow in both directions but our memory creates the illusion of one direction
The direction of time is determined by the expansion of the universe
questions.explanation: 'Some, like Huw Price, argue that the appearance of temporal flow is a cognitive illusion: the direction of time is no more intrinsic to the universe than the direction 'up' is intrinsic to space; it is a feature of our perspective, not of reality.'
questions.hint: What analogy does Price use from spatial experience?

questions.prompt: What do those who maintain 'temporal becoming' argue?
options: Time will eventually cease to exist
Any adequate metaphysics of time must account for the genuine ontological difference between what is happening now and what has already happened
Our concepts of past and future are simply grammatical conventions
Only future events have ontological reality
questions.explanation: 'Others maintain that any adequate metaphysics of time must account for the reality of temporal becoming — the genuine ontological difference between what is happening now and what has already happened.'
questions.hint: What ontological difference must be accounted for?

questions.prompt: How do neuroscientists and psychologists characterise the process of remembering?
options: As passive retrieval of stored information
As an active process of reconstruction, not a passive retrieval
As an unconscious replay of neural recordings
As reliable only for emotionally significant events
questions.explanation: 'Neuroscientists and psychologists have established that remembering is an active process of reconstruction, not a passive retrieval of stored information.'
questions.hint: What kind of process is memory, according to science?

questions.prompt: What implication does reconstructive memory have for the philosophy of time?
options: It proves that the past does not exist
If our sense of a personal past is constructed rather than retrieved, the link between subjective temporal experience and objective time structure becomes philosophically fraught
Memory is too unreliable to use as evidence in philosophy
Reconstructive memory supports the growing block universe view
questions.explanation: 'if our sense of a personal past is constructed rather than simply retrieved, the relationship between subjective temporal experience and the objective structure of time becomes even more philosophically fraught.'

questions.prompt: Why does the essay say the philosophy of time is persistently difficult?
options: Because there are too few philosophers working on it
Because language is inadequate to describe temporal phenomena
Because it sits at the intersection of metaphysics, physics, and phenomenology — three domains using different methods and standards
Because experiments on time are technically impossible
questions.explanation: 'What makes the philosophy of time so persistently difficult is that it sits at the intersection of metaphysics, physics, and phenomenology — three domains that use different methods, make different assumptions, and answer to different standards of evidence.'
questions.hint: What three domains must a complete theory of time bridge?

questions.prompt: What is the essay's conclusion about a fully adequate theory of time?
options: Physics has already provided such a theory
We do not yet have one, and some doubt we ever will, but the attempt remains among philosophy's most rewarding exercises
Presentism is the most likely candidate for a complete theory
The growing block universe has been confirmed by recent physics experiments
questions.explanation: 'We do not yet have such a theory, and there are philosophers who doubt we ever will. But the attempt to articulate one remains among the most demanding and rewarding exercises that philosophy can offer.'
questions.hint: What does the essay say about the status and value of the pursuit?

cloze.template:
Of all the phenomena that philosophy has attempted to illuminate, time is perhaps the most {{b1}} and the most resistant to analysis. We live inside it entirely; we cannot step outside it to gain perspective; and yet when we try to say with precision what it is, our concepts seem to dissolve under examination. Augustine's observation in the Confessions — 'What then is time? If no one asks me, I know; if I wish to explain it to one who asks, I know not' — remains, after sixteen centuries, as {{b2}} a summary of the problem as any.

Philosophers broadly distinguish between two questions about time: the {{b3}} question of what time fundamentally is, and the phenomenological question of how time is experienced. The two questions are connected but not identical, and some of the most productive tensions in the philosophy of time arise from the gap between them.

On the metaphysical side, the principal competing theories are presentism, eternalism, and the growing block universe. Presentism holds that only the present moment genuinely exists; the past and future are real in some {{b4}} sense — we can refer to them meaningfully, recall the past, and anticipate the future — but they do not exist as concrete entities in the way that present objects do. This view has strong intuitive support: it captures the sense that the past is gone and the future has not yet arrived.

Eternalism, by contrast, holds that past, present, and future are equally real. On this view, the distinction between past, present, and future is analogous to the distinction between here and there in space: a matter of perspective rather than of objective {{b5}} difference. The philosopher J.M.E. McTaggart introduced a famous distinction between what he called the A-series — the ordering of events as past, present, or future — and the {{b6}}-series — the ordering of events as earlier, simultaneous, or later. Eternalists typically favour the B-series, arguing that the A-series properties of 'being past' or 'being future' are {{b7}}: they describe the event's relation to the speaker's temporal location, just as 'here' describes a spatial relation to the speaker's position.

The growing block universe, associated with C.D. Broad, offers a middle position. Both the present and the past exist, but the future does not. The universe is a block of spacetime that grows as new events occur and become permanent features of the past. This view {{b8}} the intuition that the past is fixed and real while accommodating the sense that the future is genuinely open — not merely unknown but not yet determined.

Physics complicates all three views. Einstein's special theory of relativity established that {{b9}} is not absolute: two events that are simultaneous in one frame of reference need not be simultaneous in another. This finding strongly supports the B-series picture of time and poses serious difficulties for presentism, since 'the present moment' turns out not to be a well-defined global concept. The present of an observer on Earth and the present of a hypothetical observer in a distant galaxy moving at {{b10}} velocity may differ by thousands of years.

And yet the phenomenology of time is stubbornly at odds with the physicist's picture. We do not experience time as a dimension analogous to space; we experience it as flowing, as having a direction — from past to future rather than the reverse — and as having a {{b11}} moment, the present, which we inhabit and which moves forward. Philosophers call this the 'passage of time', and it is one of the most analysed and disputed topics in the field. Some, like Huw Price, argue that the appearance of temporal flow is a cognitive {{b12}}: the direction of time is no more intrinsic to the universe than the direction 'up' is intrinsic to space; it is a feature of our perspective, not of reality. Others maintain that any adequate metaphysics of time must account for the reality of temporal {{b13}} — the genuine ontological difference between what is happening now and what has already happened.

The philosophy of memory adds a further dimension. Our access to the past is not through direct perception but through memory, which is notoriously {{b14}} rather than archival. Neuroscientists have established that remembering is an active process of reconstruction, not passive retrieval. If our sense of a personal past is constructed rather than simply retrieved, the relationship between subjective temporal experience and the objective structure of time becomes even more fraught.

What makes the philosophy of time so persistently difficult is that it sits at the intersection of metaphysics, physics, and phenomenology — three domains using different methods and different standards of evidence. A fully adequate theory of time would have to be {{b15}} across all three. We do not yet have such a theory, and some philosophers doubt we ever will. But the attempt remains among the most demanding and rewarding exercises philosophy can offer.
